In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration"<ref>{{Cite web|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/|access-date=2022-02-02|website=UNESCO World Heritage Centre|language=en}}</ref> due to representing [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji's]] role as an object of worship.<ref name=":0" />
== Architecture == The shrine has a unique layout as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead has an [[:en:Altar|Altar]] where one can pray to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> The shrine was built on an axis in order to provide the best views of Mount Fuji.<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=45}}</ref>
== Yamamiya Goshinko == The "Yamamiya Goshinko" was a biannual ritual performed both among the Yamamiya Sengen-jinja and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> It is performed in April and November. During the ritual a round trip is performed between the two shrines is made the priest of the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha.<ref name=":0" /> During this trip the priest holds a spear said to be inhabited by the spirit of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Asama no Okami]], the [[kami]] of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0" /> The route that the priest followed was known as the "Goshinkomichi", the route was marked by stones markers erected in 1691.<ref name=":0" /> The final "Yamamiya Goshinko" took place during 1874 and the ritual no longer observed.<ref name=":0" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yamamiya-sengen-jinja]][[en:Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]][[ja:山宮浅間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11467749]] Wikidata name: Yamamiya Sengen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shrine in Japan}}
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yamamiya Sengen-jinja | native_name = 山宮浅間神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Yamamiya sengen1.JPG | map_type = Japan#Japan Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|138|38|13|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}}<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=Executive Summary IV}}</ref> | location = 740 Yamamiya, Fujinomiya, Shizuoka | heritage_designation = | inscriptions = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site|child=yes|Part_of=[[:en:Mount Fuji|Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration]]||Criteria=Cultural: (iii), (iv)|ID=1418}} | Heritage designation = }}
'''Yamamiya Sengen-jinja''' (山宮浅間神社) is a shrine in [[:en:Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka]],<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=39}}</ref> Japan. The shrine is unique as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead only has an altar. It uses a [[Kannabi]] instead
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration".
== History == The shrine's exact construction year is unknown however [[Archaeological excavation|archaeological digs]] have found [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] that dates back to the 12th or 15th century.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> Historical documents claim that the shrine was the predecessor to the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> The first "Yamamiya Goshinko" ritual was performed at the two shrines in the 16th century<ref name=":0" /> and the last performance of the ritual was in 1874.
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration"<ref>{{Cite web|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/|access-date=2022-02-02|website=UNESCO World Heritage Centre|language=en}}</ref> due to representing [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji's]] role as an object of worship.<ref name=":0" />
== Architecture == The shrine has a unique layout as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead has an [[:en:Altar|Altar]] where one can pray to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> The shrine was built on an axis in order to provide the best views of Mount Fuji.<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=45}}</ref>
== Yamamiya Goshinko == The "Yamamiya Goshinko" was a biannual ritual performed both among the Yamamiya Sengen-jinja and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> It is performed in April and November. During the ritual a round trip is performed between the two shrines is made the priest of the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha.<ref name=":0" /> During this trip the priest holds a spear said to be inhabited by the spirit of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Asama no Okami]], the [[kami]] of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0" /> The route that the priest followed was known as the "Goshinkomichi", the route was marked by stones markers erected in 1691.<ref name=":0" /> The final "Yamamiya Goshinko" took place during 1874 and the ritual no longer observed.<ref name=":0" />
== References == {{reflist}}{{Asama Faith}}

}}

'''Yamamiya Sengen-jinja''' (山宮浅間神社) is a shrine in [[:en:Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka]],<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=39}}</ref> Japan. The shrine is unique as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead only has an altar. It uses a [[Kannabi]] instead
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration".
== History == The shrine's exact construction year is unknown however [[Archaeological excavation|archaeological digs]] have found [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] that dates back to the 12th or 15th century.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> Historical documents claim that the shrine was the predecessor to the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> The first "Yamamiya Goshinko" ritual was performed at the two shrines in the 16th century<ref name=":0" /> and the last performance of the ritual was in 1874.
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration"<ref>{{Cite web|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/|access-date=2022-02-02|website=UNESCO World Heritage Centre|language=en}}</ref> due to representing [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji's]] role as an object of worship.<ref name=":0" />
== Architecture == The shrine has a unique layout as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead has an [[:en:Altar|Altar]] where one can pray to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> The shrine was built on an axis in order to provide the best views of Mount Fuji.<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=45}}</ref>
== Yamamiya Goshinko == The "Yamamiya Goshinko" was a biannual ritual performed both among the Yamamiya Sengen-jinja and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> It is performed in April and November. During the ritual a round trip is performed between the two shrines is made the priest of the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha.<ref name=":0" /> During this trip the priest holds a spear said to be inhabited by the spirit of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Asama no Okami]], the [[kami]] of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0" /> The route that the priest followed was known as the "Goshinkomichi", the route was marked by stones markers erected in 1691.<ref name=":0" /> The final "Yamamiya Goshinko" took place during 1874 and the ritual no longer observed.<ref name=":0" />
== References == {{reflist}}{{Asama Faith}}

== History == The shrine's exact construction year is unknown however [[Archaeological excavation|archaeological digs]] have found [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] that dates back to the 12th or 15th century.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> Historical documents claim that the shrine was the predecessor to the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> The first "Yamamiya Goshinko" ritual was performed at the two shrines in the 16th century<ref name=":0" /> and the last performance of the ritual was in 1874.
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration"<ref>{{Cite web|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/|access-date=2022-02-02|website=UNESCO World Heritage Centre|language=en}}</ref> due to representing [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji's]] role as an object of worship.<ref name=":0" />
== Architecture == The shrine has a unique layout as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead has an [[:en:Altar|Altar]] where one can pray to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> The shrine was built on an axis in order to provide the best views of Mount Fuji.<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=45}}</ref>
== Yamamiya Goshinko == The "Yamamiya Goshinko" was a biannual ritual performed both among the Yamamiya Sengen-jinja and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> It is performed in April and November. During the ritual a round trip is performed between the two shrines is made the priest of the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha.<ref name=":0" /> During this trip the priest holds a spear said to be inhabited by the spirit of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Asama no Okami]], the [[kami]] of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0" /> The route that the priest followed was known as the "Goshinkomichi", the route was marked by stones markers erected in 1691.<ref name=":0" /> The final "Yamamiya Goshinko" took place during 1874 and the ritual no longer observed.<ref name=":0" />

During the ritual a round trip is performed between the two shrines is made the priest of the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha.<ref name=":0" /> During this trip the priest holds a spear said to be inhabited by the spirit of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Asama no Okami]], the [[kami]] of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0" /> The route that the priest followed was known as the "Goshinkomichi", the route was marked by stones markers erected in 1691.<ref name=":0" /> The final "Yamamiya Goshinko" took place during 1874 and the ritual no longer observed.<ref name=":0" />

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yamamiya Sengen-jinja | native_name = 山宮浅間神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Yamamiya sengen1.JPG | map_type = Japan#Japan Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|138|38|13|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}}<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=Executive Summary IV}}</ref> | location = 740 Yamamiya, Fujinomiya, Shizuoka | heritage_designation = | inscriptions = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site|child=yes|Part_of=[[:en:Mount Fuji|Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration]]||Criteria=Cultural: (iii), (iv)|ID=1418}} | Heritage designation = }}
'''Yamamiya Sengen-jinja''' (山宮浅間神社) is a shrine in [[:en:Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka]],<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=39}}</ref> Japan. The shrine is unique as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead only has an altar. It uses a [[Kannabi]] instead
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration".
== History == The shrine's exact construction year is unknown however [[Archaeological excavation|archaeological digs]] have found [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] that dates back to the 12th or 15th century.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> Historical documents claim that the shrine was the predecessor to the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> The first "Yamamiya Goshinko" ritual was performed at the two shrines in the 16th century<ref name=":0" /> and the last performance of the ritual was in 1874.
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration"<ref>{{Cite web|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/|access-date=2022-02-02|website=UNESCO World Heritage Centre|language=en}}</ref> due to representing [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji's]] role as an object of worship.<ref name=":0" />
== Architecture == The shrine has a unique layout as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead has an [[:en:Altar|Altar]] where one can pray to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> The shrine was built on an axis in order to provide the best views of Mount Fuji.<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=45}}</ref>
== Yamamiya Goshinko == The "Yamamiya Goshinko" was a biannual ritual performed both among the Yamamiya Sengen-jinja and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> It is performed in April and November. During the ritual a round trip is performed between the two shrines is made the priest of the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha.<ref name=":0" /> During this trip the priest holds a spear said to be inhabited by the spirit of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Asama no Okami]], the [[kami]] of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]].<ref name=":0" /> The route that the priest followed was known as the "Goshinkomichi", the route was marked by stones markers erected in 1691.<ref name=":0" /> The final "Yamamiya Goshinko" took place during 1874 and the ritual no longer observed.<ref name=":0" />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yamamiya-sengen-jinja]][[en:Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]][[ja:山宮浅間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yamamiya-sengen-jinja]][[en:Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]][[ja:山宮浅間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yamamiya-sengen-jinja]][[en:Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]][[ja:山宮浅間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11467749]] Wikidata name: Yamamiya Sengen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shrine in Japan}}
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yamamiya Sengen-jinja | native_name = 山宮浅間神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Yamamiya sengen1.JPG | map_type = Japan#Japan Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|138|38|13|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}}<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=Executive Summary IV}}</ref> | location = 740 Yamamiya, Fujinomiya, Shizuoka | heritage_designation = | inscriptions = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site|child=yes|Part_of={{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration}}||Criteria=Cultural: (iii), (iv)|ID=1418}} | Heritage designation = }}
'''Yamamiya Sengen-jinja''' (山宮浅間神社) is a shrine in {{ill|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|en|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|WD=Q496072|lt=Fujinomiya, Shizuoka}},<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=39}}</ref> Japan. The shrine is unique as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead only has an altar. It uses a [[Kannabi]] instead
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}} "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration".
== History == The shrine's exact construction year is unknown however [[Archaeological excavation|archaeological digs]] have found {{ill|Earthenware|en|Earthenware|WD=Q1377111|lt=Earthenware}} that dates back to the 12th or 15th century.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> Historical documents claim that the shrine was the predecessor to the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> The first "Yamamiya Goshinko" ritual was performed at the two shrines in the 16th century<ref name=":0" /> and the last performance of the ritual was in 1874.
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}} "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration"<ref>{{Cite web|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/|access-date=2022-02-02|website=UNESCO World Heritage Centre|language=en}}</ref> due to representing {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji's}} role as an object of worship.<ref name=":0" />
== Architecture == The shrine has a unique layout as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead has an {{ill|Altar|en|Altar|WD=Q101687|lt=Altar}} where one can pray to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> The shrine was built on an axis in order to provide the best views of Mount Fuji.<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=45}}</ref>
== Yamamiya Goshinko == The "Yamamiya Goshinko" was a biannual ritual performed both among the Yamamiya Sengen-jinja and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> It is performed in April and November. During the ritual a round trip is performed between the two shrines is made the priest of the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha.<ref name=":0" /> During this trip the priest holds a spear said to be inhabited by the spirit of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Asama no Okami]], the [[kami]] of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}.<ref name=":0" /> The route that the priest followed was known as the "Goshinkomichi", the route was marked by stones markers erected in 1691.<ref name=":0" /> The final "Yamamiya Goshinko" took place during 1874 and the ritual no longer observed.<ref name=":0" />
== References == {{reflist}}{{Asama Faith}}
[[en:Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]]
<!-- <ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> -->

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yamamiya-sengen-jinja]][[en:Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]][[ja:山宮浅間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yamamiya-sengen-jinja]][[en:Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]][[ja:山宮浅間神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yamamiya-sengen-jinja]][[en:Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]][[ja:山宮浅間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11467749]] Wikidata name: Yamamiya Sengen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shrine in Japan}}
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yamamiya Sengen-jinja | native_name = 山宮浅間神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Yamamiya sengen1.JPG | map_type = Japan#Japan Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|138|38|13|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}}<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=Executive Summary IV}}</ref> | location = 740 Yamamiya, Fujinomiya, Shizuoka | heritage_designation = | inscriptions = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site|child=yes|Part_of={{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration}}||Criteria=Cultural: (iii), (iv)|ID=1418}} | Heritage designation = }}
'''Yamamiya Sengen-jinja''' (山宮浅間神社) is a shrine in {{ill|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|en|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|WD=Q496072|lt=Fujinomiya, Shizuoka}},<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=39}}</ref> Japan. The shrine is unique as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead only has an altar. It uses a [[Kannabi]] instead
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}} "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration".
== History == The shrine's exact construction year is unknown however [[Archaeological excavation|archaeological digs]] have found {{ill|Earthenware|en|Earthenware|WD=Q1377111|lt=Earthenware}} that dates back to the 12th or 15th century.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> Historical documents claim that the shrine was the predecessor to the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> The first "Yamamiya Goshinko" ritual was performed at the two shrines in the 16th century<ref name=":0" /> and the last performance of the ritual was in 1874.
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}} "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration"<ref>{{Cite web|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/|access-date=2022-02-02|website=UNESCO World Heritage Centre|language=en}}</ref> due to representing {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji's}} role as an object of worship.<ref name=":0" />
== Architecture == The shrine has a unique layout as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead has an {{ill|Altar|en|Altar|WD=Q101687|lt=Altar}} where one can pray to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> The shrine was built on an axis in order to provide the best views of Mount Fuji.<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=45}}</ref>
== Yamamiya Goshinko == The "Yamamiya Goshinko" was a biannual ritual performed both among the Yamamiya Sengen-jinja and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> It is performed in April and November. During the ritual a round trip is performed between the two shrines is made the priest of the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha.<ref name=":0" /> During this trip the priest holds a spear said to be inhabited by the spirit of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Asama no Okami]], the [[kami]] of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}.<ref name=":0" /> The route that the priest followed was known as the "Goshinkomichi", the route was marked by stones markers erected in 1691.<ref name=":0" /> The final "Yamamiya Goshinko" took place during 1874 and the ritual no longer observed.<ref name=":0" />
== References == {{reflist}}{{Asama Faith}}
[[en:Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]]
<!-- <ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> -->

{{Short description|Shrine in Japan}}
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yamamiya Sengen-jinja | native_name = 山宮浅間神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = Shinto | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Yamamiya sengen1.JPG | map_type = Japan#Japan Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|138|38|13|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}}<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=Executive Summary IV}}</ref> | location = 740 Yamamiya, Fujinomiya, Shizuoka | heritage_designation = | inscriptions = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site|child=yes|Part_of={{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration|1=Mount Fuji}}||Criteria=Cultural: (iii), (iv)|ID=1418}} | Heritage designation = }}
'''Yamamiya Sengen-jinja''' (山宮浅間神社) is a shrine in {{ill|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|en|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|WD=Q496072|lt=Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|1=Fujinomiya, Shizuoka}},<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=39}}</ref> Japan. The shrine is unique as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead only has an altar. It uses a [[Kannabi]] instead
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}} "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration".
== History == The shrine's exact construction year is unknown however [[Archaeological excavation|archaeological digs]] have found {{ill|Earthenware|en|Earthenware|WD=Q1377111|lt=Earthenware|1=Earthenware}} that dates back to the 12th or 15th century.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> Historical documents claim that the shrine was the predecessor to the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> The first "Yamamiya Goshinko" ritual was performed at the two shrines in the 16th century<ref name=":0" /> and the last performance of the ritual was in 1874.
In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}} "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration"<ref>{{Cite web|title=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1418/|access-date=2022-02-02|website=UNESCO World Heritage Centre|language=en}}</ref> due to representing {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji's|1=Mount Fuji}} role as an object of worship.<ref name=":0" />
== Architecture == The shrine has a unique layout as it lacks a [[honden]] and instead has an {{ill|Altar|en|Altar|WD=Q101687|lt=Altar|1=Altar}} where one can pray to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=88}}</ref> The shrine was built on an axis in order to provide the best views of Mount Fuji.<ref>{{Cite web|date=January 2012|title=Nomination of Fujisan for Inscription on the World Heritage List|url=http://whc.unesco.org/uploads/nominations/1418.pdf|access-date=2022-01-24|website=UNESCO heritage centre|page=45}}</ref>
== Yamamiya Goshinko == The "Yamamiya Goshinko" was a biannual ritual performed both among the Yamamiya Sengen-jinja and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]].<ref name=":0" /> It is performed in April and November. During the ritual a round trip is performed between the two shrines is made the priest of the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha.<ref name=":0" /> During this trip the priest holds a spear said to be inhabited by the spirit of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Asama no Okami]], the [[kami]] of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}.<ref name=":0" /> The route that the priest followed was known as the "Goshinkomichi", the route was marked by stones markers erected in 1691.<ref name=":0" /> The final "Yamamiya Goshinko" took place during 1874 and the ritual no longer observed.<ref name=":0" />
== References == {{reflist}}{{Asama Faith}}
[[en:Yamamiya Sengen Shrine]]
